explanation:
**Athletic training** combines science (physiology, biomechanics) and practice. Intensity zones, progressive load, rest, and nutrition form the complete system.

## How It's Calculated

Recommended rest days after a marathon, calculated using the 1-day-per-kilometer recovery formula.

This calculator applies the formula with your input values, checking typical ranges and alerting you to any values outside normal parameters.

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guideline only. For critical decisions (medical, training-related), consult with a professional coach or sports medicine specialist. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically.

faq.q: How many days should I rest after running a marathon?
faq.a: A common guideline is taking 1 rest day per kilometer of race distance. For a full marathon (42.2 km), that means about 3 weeks of recovery with easy running or complete rest.

faq.q: What does the 1 day per km rule mean?
faq.a: This formula, developed by running coach Jack Daniels, suggests one recovery day for each kilometer raced. It's a practical way to estimate how long your body needs to bounce back.

faq.q: What counts as an 'easy' run after a marathon?
faq.a: Very light jogging or walking—sustainable at a conversational pace with no speed work or intervals. The focus is movement, not effort.

faq.q: Is it okay to run the day after a marathon?
faq.a: Most coaches recommend taking that first day completely off. Light walking and stretching are fine, but running should wait until your body has had genuine rest.

faq.q: How long until I can do hard workouts again?
faq.a: Recovery is gradual. After your initial rest period, spend several weeks building back to normal mileage, then slowly reintroduce speed work and intervals.

faq.q: How much time should pass before I run another marathon?
faq.a: A minimum of 16-20 weeks between marathons gives your body adequate recovery and time to prepare properly for the next race.

faq.q: Is this calculator free to use?
faq.a: Completely free—no account, no login, no hidden costs.

faq.q: Are these recovery timelines accurate for everyone?
faq.a: These are science-based guidelines that work for most runners, but individual recovery depends on fitness, age, and experience. If you have concerns, talk to a running coach or sports medicine professional.

faq.q: Will you store my personal information?
faq.a: No. Calculations run locally in your browser—nothing is sent to our servers or saved.
